behaviors.description: Parser rejects invalid cron expressions with descriptive error messages
behaviors.justification: This is a pure parsing function with deterministic input/output. No I/O, network, subprocess, or LLM involved. The real failure would be: parser accepts an invalid expression or returns a non-descriptive error message. Both caught by unit testing the parser function directly against invalid inputs (month=13, day=32, malformed syntax). Mocks cannot hide this failure since the entire logic is in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Create unit tests that pass invalid cron expressions to the parser (invalid month values 13-99, invalid day values 32-99, malformed syntax like missing fields, non-numeric values) and verify the parser rejects each with a clear, actionable error message indicating what is wrong. Use parametrized tests to cover the permutation of invalid month/day/hour/minute/weekday combinations.

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard and edge-case cron expressions
behaviors.justification: This is a pure algorithmic function that computes timestamps from a cron expression and start time. No external I/O or subprocess. Real failures: off-by-one errors in date arithmetic, incorrect handling of month boundaries, incorrect leap year logic, wrong timestamp for 'last day of month' or 'nearest weekday' logic. All testable with deterministic inputs. Mocks cannot hide algorithmic bugs since the output is directly verifiable against known calendar facts.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test the calculator with known-good cron expressions and verify outputs against expected timestamps. Include: (1) simple recurring times (e.g., '0 9 * * *' = 9 AM every day), (2) last day of month ('0 0 L * *' or equivalent), (3) nearest weekday semantics, (4) leap year boundaries, (5) month boundaries (Jan 31 → Feb 1), (6) year boundaries (Dec 31 → Jan 1). Assert exact timestamps match expected values. Use property-based testing to verify the next-run function is monotonically increasing.

behaviors.description: Overlap detector identifies when two schedules would fire within the configured minimum gap
behaviors.justification: This is a pure comparison/interval-overlap function. Takes two schedule objects and a gap threshold, returns boolean + conflicting windows. No I/O, no external dependencies. Real failure: off-by-one error in gap calculation (e.g., 5-minute gap allowed but 5-minute actual gap triggers false positive), or failure to return the conflicting window. All testable with deterministic inputs (schedule objects + integer gap). Mocks cannot hide interval-arithmetic bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test the overlap detector with pairs of cron schedules and gap configurations. Test cases: (1) Two schedules firing 3 minutes apart with 5-minute gap threshold (should trigger overlap, return the 3-minute window), (2) Two schedules firing 6 minutes apart with 5-minute gap threshold (should NOT trigger), (3) Two schedules with identical fire times (should trigger overlap), (4) Two non-overlapping schedules (should not trigger). Verify: boolean result is correct AND the reported conflicting time window is accurate.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across a DST transition
behaviors.justification: This is a pure function test, but with a critical constraint: MUST use real timezone library (pytz/zoneinfo), not mocks. Real failures: (1) scheduler computes wrong local time during spring-forward gap (2:00-3:00 AM missing), (2) scheduler mishandles ambiguous hour during fall-back, (3) scheduler returns naive datetime instead of aware. A mock timezone library could hide these failures by returning incorrect offsets. Using real timezone libraries at Unit level forces correctness. No system I/O or subprocess needed—timezone data is bundled with Python.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test the scheduler's next-run-time function using real pytz/zoneinfo (not mocked). Test cases with actual DST transition dates: (1) Job scheduled for 2:30 AM on US/Eastern spring-forward day (2024-03-10: 2:00-3:00 AM does not exist)—verify the scheduler either skips to 3:30 AM or raises a clear error, (2) Job scheduled for 1:30 AM on US/Eastern fall-back day (2024-11-03: 1:00-2:00 AM occurs twice)—verify the scheduler handles ambiguity (e.g., picks first or second occurrence per policy). Assert timezone info is preserved in output (aware datetime, correct tzinfo). Do NOT mock datetime.now() or timezone functions.

behaviors.description: Missed-run policy determines whether to skip or backfill when computed run time has already passed
behaviors.justification: This is a pure conditional logic function. Takes (next_run_time, current_time, policy_setting) and returns a decision (skip vs. backfill). No I/O, network, or external state. Real failures: policy is misinterpreted (skip interpreted as backfill, or vice versa), or edge cases missed (e.g., policy='skip' but engine still schedules a backfill run). All testable with deterministic inputs. Mocks cannot hide conditional logic bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test the missed-run policy logic with deterministic scenarios. Test cases: (1) next_run_time is 1 hour ago, policy='skip'—verify no backfill is scheduled, (2) next_run_time is 1 hour ago, policy='backfill'—verify engine schedules runs for missed intervals (e.g., every 10 minutes for the past hour = 6 runs), (3) next_run_time is in the future—verify normal scheduling regardless of policy, (4) next_run_time is 'now'—verify handled correctly (no skip/backfill needed). For backfill, verify the count of backfilled runs matches the interval. Use parametrized tests for policy variations and past-time offsets.
